title: Neuropsychological effects of electroconvulsive therapy
abstract:
NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL EFFECTS OF ELECTROCONVULSIVE 
THERAPY' 
STEVEN G. GOLDSTEIN 
Northside Community Mental Health Center, Tampa, Florida 
SUSAN B. FILSKOV 
LELON A. WEAVER AND JOHN 0. IVES 
C'niversity of South Florida 
Although ECT as the treatment of choice for psychotic de ression has been 
in use for many years, little is known about the neocorticafresidual of such 
treatments inferred from behavioral measures. The ma'or portion of the 
literature has been concerned with inferred or observed changes in affective 
state. The present study compared pre- and posttreatment performances on 
the Halstead-Reitan neuropsychological battery of 20 patients who were re- 
ceiving ECT from two different machines. Most Ss gave indicators of cerebral 
impairment prior to treatment when performance of one side of the body was 
contrasted with performance of the other side. After treatment, there was an 
increased number of Ss who evidenced signs consistent with damage to the 
right cerebral hemisphere. Some concern was raised that a large number of pa- 
tients who eventually are subjected to ECT because of depression behave in 
this way because of an undiagnosed neocortical dysfunction. There is some 
suggestion that the effect of the procedure is to either create or intensify a 
right hemisphere focus as inferred from behavioral measures. 
University of Vermont 
The use of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) long has been an emotion-laden 
issue in regard to its effectiveness and hypothesized injury to the brain among both 
professional and lay groups. The most recent example of this type of discussion 
was by Friedberg (1975), who argued (although he cites no references) that ECT as 
a treatment procedure was probably worse than no treatment at all. He suggested 
that the effects of the treatment were more akin to aversion therapies than they 
were to the more positive procedures suggested by other authors (Hurwitz, 1974). 
These authors have suggested that ECT is effective with little harm beyond tran- 
sient loss of memory for recent events. 
Studies of the effects of ECT with standardized psychological tests have 
tended to support the latter view (Dornbush, 1972; Harper & Weins, 1975). Camp- 
bell's (1961) review of the psychological effects of ECT summarizes this literature 
to that point. Most studies in this area since that time have suggested that in- 
tellectual function (as measured by the Wechsler scales) has been relatively un- 
affected by ECT treatments whether delivered singularly or in series. Immediate 
memory has been affected (as noted by such tests as the Wechsler Memory Scale), 
and a number of studies have noted the production of a retrograde amnesia. These 
results are not surprising in their consistency. The Wechsler scales are relatively 
good indicators of the ability to draw on experiences learned some time in the past 
and have not been shown to be effective measures of recent memory (Goldstein, 
Kleinknecht, & Gallo, 1970). 
There have been relatively few studies over the past years that have dealt 
with the less popular psychological measures. Some of these measures give behavio- 
ral indices that are related directly to neocortical function. Early neuropsychologi- 
cal studies with unilateral placement of electrodes noted that placement on the 
left side sometimes resulted in dysphasic signs (D'Elia, 1972). As there seemed no 
real behavioral difference on outcome measure as a function of the side of place- 
ment, it followed that placement was preferable on the right side so as to minimize 
effects that were measured by neuropsychological assessment techniques. 
'This research was supported in part by USPHS Grant 5 ROl MH 24105. Reprint requests 
should be sent to Steven G. Goldstein, Northside Community Mental Health Center, 13301 North 
30th Street, Tampa, Florida 33612. 
798 
 Neuropsychological E$ects 
799 
Neuropsychological disturbances that resulted from right hemisphere place- 
ment were reported to involve mainly visuo-spatial perception (Small, Small, 
Milstein & Moore, 1972; Small, Small, Milstein & Sharpley, 1973). Such a finding 
is not surprising in view of our traditional understanding of the various skills 
mediated by the separate hemispheres of the brain. 
Although some studies have purported to investigate the neuropsychological 
effects of ECT, the measurement of behavior has not been very extensive or con- 
ducted with standardized tests. None of these studies has administered an ex- 
tensive, standardized (Reitan, 1966) and validated (Filskov & Goldstein, 1974) 
battery of neuropsychological tests. The present report describes what effect, if 
any, a series of ECT treatments had on patients who were so examined with this 
neuropsychological testing procedure. 
METHOD 
Subjects 
The 20 patients who are considered in this paper were those whom we were 
able to test prior to ECT and then again immediately after the final treatment 
of the series. Eleven were assigned at random to the group that received the tradi- 
tional series of treatment (TRAD) with a standard AC machine, and 11 were 
placed in the group that received the ECT series from a low-energy brief-stimulus 
machine (LEBS). In the TRAD group, 1 S was untestable and a second refused 
follow-up testing. Data were thus available on a total of 20 Ss, 9 TRAD and 11 
LEBS Ss, respectively. 
Three Ss in each group had histories of prior ECT although the records of 
these people were not as complete as we might have wished. The number of series 
of prior treatments is unclear, and a review of their histories seems to indicate that, 
at the very least, they must be considered a heterogeneous group. The data for 
1 patient certainly are complicated by the fact that she had known multiple sclerosis. 
Tables 1 and 2 present data for the subgroups along with indices for the merged 
groups in regard to characteristics of, and treatments given to, the S groups.

The criteria for patient selection for either of the treatment groups were that 
the patient volunteered to undergo such a treatment and was diagnosed as having 
an endogenous (psychotic) depression. Most of the patients had an adequate and 
unsuccessful trial of antidepressant medicat,ions although some patients who re- 
quired rapid treatment results or who had records of previous, very successful 
ECT, were not given antidepressant medication first. Finally, some patients had 
been judged so deeply depressed that waiting for possible medication effects to 
develop constituted a hazard or unjustifiable withholding of a treatment known to 
be efficacious for the condition.
